cause: Print temperature too high
fix: Lower nozzle temperature by 5°C increments until stringing stops. Run a temperature tower test to find the optimal value.

cause: Insufficient retraction
fix: Increase retraction distance (start with 1mm for direct drive, 4-6mm for Bowden) and retraction speed (40-60mm/s). Enable 'combing' or 'avoid crossing perimeters' in slicer.

cause: Travel speed too low
fix: Increase travel speed to 150-200mm/s so the nozzle moves quickly between points, reducing ooze time.

cause: PETG is inherently stringy due to its viscous melt
fix: Use lower temps (220-230°C), enable wipe/coasting, increase retraction to 4-5mm. Some stringing is normal with PETG — use a heat gun to clean up.

cause: TPU's flexibility makes retraction ineffective
fix: Minimize retraction (0.5-2mm max on direct drive, avoid on Bowden). Lower temp to minimum printable range. Reduce travel distance via slicer settings.

cause: Insufficient bed adhesion
fix: Clean bed with IPA, apply adhesive (glue stick, hairspray), ensure proper first layer squish. Level the bed carefully.

cause: Uneven cooling causes differential contraction
fix: Use a brim (5-10mm) to increase bed contact area. Reduce part cooling fan speed for the first 3-5 layers. Avoid drafts near the printer.

cause: Bed temperature too low or cooling too fast
fix: Increase bed temperature by 5-10°C. Slow down first layer speed to 20mm/s for better adhesion.

cause: ABS contracts significantly when cooling, causing corners to lift
fix: Use enclosure (mandatory for ABS), set bed to 100-110°C, disable part cooling fan entirely, use wide brim or raft. ABS slurry (ABS dissolved in acetone) on the bed helps.

cause: Nylon shrinks significantly during cooling
fix: Use enclosure, apply PVA glue stick to bed, set bed to 70-90°C, use raft for large prints. Ensure filament is completely dry before printing.

cause: Polycarbonate has high thermal contraction
fix: Enclosure mandatory (maintain 50°C+ chamber temp). Bed at 110-120°C. Use Magigoo PC or PEI sheet. Print with raft for parts wider than 100mm.

cause: Print temperature too low for material
fix: Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10°C. Each material has a sweet spot where layers fuse well — too low causes delamination.

cause: Part cooling fan too high
fix: Reduce fan speed. Over-cooling prevents layers from bonding. For materials like ABS, ASA, and Nylon, use 0% fan or minimal fan.

cause: Layer height too large for nozzle diameter
fix: Layer height should not exceed 75% of nozzle diameter (e.g., max 0.3mm for a 0.4mm nozzle). Reduce layer height for better bonding.

cause: Moisture-absorbed Nylon produces steam that weakens layer bonds
fix: Dry filament at 70-80°C for 6-12 hours before printing. Use a dry box during printing. Wet Nylon pops and crackles — if you hear this, stop and dry.

cause: PETG needs higher temps than PLA for proper fusion
fix: Print at 235-245°C. Reduce fan to 30-50%. PETG achieves best layer adhesion at higher temps with moderate cooling.

cause: First layer too squished against the bed
fix: Increase Z-offset slightly (move nozzle further from bed by 0.02-0.05mm). Calibrate first layer height with a test print.

cause: Bed temperature too high softening the first layers
fix: Lower bed temperature by 5-10°C. Use 'elephant foot compensation' setting in slicer (insets first layer perimeter by 0.1-0.2mm).

cause: First layer printed too slowly allowing excess heat buildup
fix: Increase first layer speed slightly (from 20 to 25-30mm/s) to reduce heat exposure time on the first layer.

cause: Heat creep — filament softens too far up in the heatbreak
fix: Ensure hotend fan (not part cooling fan) is running at 100%. Check for dust/debris in the cooling fins. Reduce retraction distance to prevent molten filament climbing up.

cause: Debris or carbonized filament blocking the nozzle
fix: Perform a cold pull (heat to printing temp, push filament through, cool to 90°C for PLA / 160°C for Nylon, then pull sharply). Repeat 3-5 times.

cause: Printing temperature too low causing incomplete melting
fix: Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10°C. Ensure temperature is stable (PID tune the hotend if it fluctuates more than ±2°C).

cause: PVA absorbs moisture rapidly and swells, blocking the nozzle
fix: Dry PVA at 45-50°C for 4-6 hours. Store in sealed container with desiccant at all times. Only load PVA immediately before printing.

cause: Wet Nylon produces steam bubbles that create jams
fix: Dry at 70-80°C for 6-12 hours. Use inline dry box. Listen for popping/crackling — if present, filament is wet.

cause: Extruder tension too low or extruder gear worn
fix: Tighten extruder idler spring. Check drive gear teeth for wear — replace if teeth are rounded. Clean gear teeth of filament shavings.

cause: Partial clog restricting flow
fix: Perform cold pull to clear partial blockage. If persistent, replace nozzle. Check PTFE tube for deformation at the hot end.

cause: Incorrect flow rate or E-steps
fix: Calibrate E-steps: mark filament at 100mm, extrude 100mm, measure. Adjust steps/mm in firmware. Then fine-tune flow rate with a single-wall cube.

cause: Flexible filament buckling between extruder gear and hotend
fix: Use direct drive extruder (not Bowden). Print slowly (20-30mm/s). Ensure filament path has no gaps where TPU can escape sideways.

cause: Flow rate/extrusion multiplier set too high
fix: Reduce flow rate to 95% and test. Calibrate by printing a single-wall cube — measure wall thickness and adjust multiplier until it matches slicer line width.

cause: Filament diameter inconsistent or thicker than expected
fix: Measure filament diameter at 5+ points with calipers. Enter the average diameter in the slicer (default 1.75mm may not match). Variance over ±0.05mm indicates low-quality filament.

cause: Nozzle temperature too high causing excessive flow
fix: Lower temperature by 5-10°C. Over-heated filament becomes too fluid and oozes excess material. Run a temperature tower to find the optimal setting.

cause: PETG is more fluid than PLA and over-extrudes easily at default settings
fix: Reduce flow rate to 92-95% for PETG. Lower temperature to 225-235°C. PETG is forgiving of slight under-extrusion, which actually improves surface quality.
